summary: Japan was my father's homeland, and it played a big role in our family's story. My dad spoke English but dressed like an old Japanese farmer during the American occupation of Japan after World War II. Later, when my dad became bitter about the treatment we received in the U.S., he wanted to return to Japan, saying it was his country where such things wouldn’t happen again. I, however, was born and raised in America and didn’t want to go back. I also attended Japanese school growing up, which helped me learn the Japanese language, though I’m starting to forget it now.

During the American occupation of Japan, I remember my grandfather surprising the American occupation people because he spoke English despite dressing like an old Japanese farmer. This was after World War II, and it showed how my family bridged both cultures. The occupation period was a significant time that connected my family’s Japanese roots with their American experiences.

My dad worked in Seattle at a jewelry store while also being a concert violinist, which was quite unusual. My mom was the piano accompanist, so music was a big part of their lives even back then. Seattle was an early chapter in my family’s story before we moved to Los Angeles.

We moved to Los Angeles where my dad opened a fruit and vegetable stand on Sunset Boulevard in Hollywood. My mom worked as a domestic, and we lived in a mostly Caucasian neighborhood with a few Japanese families. Growing up in L.A. was comfortable, and I even knew some notable people like Anna May Wong and John Aiso who lived nearby. It was a nice community, and I felt I could play with kids of all backgrounds.

Anna May Wong was a famous Chinese movie star who lived on my street in Hollywood. It was interesting to have someone so well-known as a neighbor during my childhood. I remember mentioning her because she was part of the small but diverse community we lived in.

John Aiso was a family friend who lived just a couple of doors up from us in Hollywood. He later became the judge for O.J. Simpson, which was quite notable. Having him nearby made our neighborhood feel connected to important figures in the Japanese American community.

O.J. Simpson was connected to my neighborhood through John Aiso, who was the judge in his case. Although I didn’t know O.J. personally, the fact that John Aiso lived nearby and was involved in such a high-profile case stood out to me when I looked back on those times.

I remember the day Pearl Harbor was bombed in December 1941 very clearly. I was flying model airplanes with my Caucasian friends in Gardena when someone told us the Japs bombed Pearl Harbor. I didn’t even know where Pearl Harbor was at first, but the news changed everything. Suddenly, I felt like the enemy even though I was American, and school became a difficult place with kids calling me 'Jap.' It was a terrible and confusing time for me.

My dad blamed President Franklin D. Roosevelt for the tensions with Japan because of the embargoes and policies that were hard on Japan before the war. He felt Roosevelt’s actions contributed to what happened at Pearl Harbor and the subsequent treatment of Japanese Americans. This was something my dad told me when I was young, reflecting his bitterness about those times.

My uncle had a Ford car with a rumble seat, and I loved riding in it to the beach. Sitting in the rumble seat catching the air was one of my fond childhood memories. It was a simple pleasure that stood out during those years before the war disrupted our lives.

When Executive Order 9066 was issued, my family had to prepare to leave our home and give up my dad’s business, which was very hard for him. My mom was busy sorting and trying to sell our belongings, but people often offered peanuts for our stuff or even broke our Japanese dish sets. We had to leave everything behind and go to camp, which was a terrible experience. It was a turning point that changed our lives forever.

My family went straight to Manzanar from the Hollywood area because my uncle lived nearby and his group was going there. We chose to go with him to avoid moving twice, which was smart. I remember being on a Greyhound bus with the shades pulled down, not understanding why we had to hide. Manzanar was where we spent the internment period during the war.

Our group was originally supposed to go to the Santa Anita Assembly Center, but my family opted to go straight to Manzanar with my uncle’s group to avoid moving twice. I didn’t actually stay at Santa Anita, but it was part of the relocation process for many Japanese Americans. It was a temporary place before the camps like Manzanar.

We were transported to the internment camps on a Greyhound bus, and I remember that they pulled the shades down so people outside couldn’t see us. I wasn’t sure if they didn’t want us to see them or vice versa, but it felt strange and secretive. That bus ride marked the beginning of a very difficult chapter in our lives.

I grew up bilingual, speaking both English and Japanese at home. My parents made sure I went to Japanese school every Saturday, which I hated at the time because all the other kids were playing. The Japanese school was strict, like in Japan, with rulers and discipline, but it helped me learn the language. Later, when I went to Japan, knowing Japanese was useful, though now I’m starting to forget it since I don’t have anyone to speak with.
There was no mention of Hewlett-Packard in my story or experiences. It didn’t come up in my memories or the events I lived through.
The Medal of Honor was not part of my personal story or the events I described. It didn’t come up in my recollections or family history.
